title: try/catch тАФ contain the crash
expectedOutput: Caught: Unexpected token 't', "{ this is not"... is not valid JSON
successMessage: Without try/catch, that JSON.parse crash would end your program. With it, you handle the error and carry on. Every JSON.parse should be wrapped in try/catch.

title: The Error object
instruction:
When an error is caught, you get an `Error` object with:
- `error.message` тАФ human-readable description
- `error.name` тАФ the error type ('TypeError', 'SyntaxError', etc.)
- `error.stack` тАФ full call stack (for debugging)

Inspect a real error.
successMessage: Every Error is also instanceof Error тАФ they all extend the base Error class. error.name tells you the type. error.message tells you what went wrong.

title: Built-in error types
instruction:
JavaScript has 7 built-in error types тАФ each for a different category:

- `SyntaxError` тАФ invalid code
- `TypeError` тАФ wrong type operation
- `ReferenceError` тАФ undefined variable
- `RangeError` тАФ value out of range
- `URIError` тАФ malformed URI
- `EvalError` тАФ eval problems (rare)
- `Error` тАФ generic base

Trigger and catch three different types.
expectedOutput: TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'property')
successMessage: Three types, three different error names. Checking error.name (or instanceof) lets you handle each type differently тАФ retry for network errors, show validation message for TypeError.

title: throw тАФ trigger your own errors
instruction:
`throw` lets you trigger an error yourself тАФ to signal invalid input or impossible state.

You can throw any value, but best practice is to throw an `Error` object so you get a stack trace.
hint: if (amount < 0) throw new Error('Amount cannot be negative');
expectedOutput: TypeError: Amount must be a number, got string
RangeError: Amount must be positive, got -10
successMessage: Four cases тАФ three throws, one success. Throwing specific error types (TypeError, RangeError) lets callers catch and handle them differently.

title: Custom error classes
instruction:
Extend `Error` to create your own error types тАФ makes it easy to catch and handle your application's specific errors.

Add the missing `ValidationError` and `NetworkError` classes.
hint: class ValidationError extends Error { constructor(field, message) { super(message); this.name = 'ValidationError'; this.field = field; } }
successMessage: ValidationError extends AppError extends Error тАФ the chain goes three levels. catch (e) { if (e instanceof ValidationError) ... } тАФ catch specific types and handle them differently.

title: finally тАФ always runs
expectedOutput: Query failed: Query cannot be empty
successMessage: 'Closing connection.' always prints тАФ even when an error occurs. Database connections, file handles, loading spinners тАФ all should be closed in finally.

title: Re-throw тАФ only handle what you know
hint: if (e instanceof ValidationError) { ... } else { throw e; }
expectedOutput: Caught outer: undefinedFunction is not defined

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Re-throwing is responsible error handling. Only catch what you understand тАФ let unexpected errors bubble up where they can be logged and investigated.

title: Build a safe API call wrapper
instruction:
Final challenge тАФ no starter code.

Write `safeCall(fn, ...args)` тАФ a wrapper that:
1. Calls `fn(...args)` inside try/catch
2. Returns `{ success: true, data: result }` on success
3. Returns `{ success: false, error: err.message, type: err.name }` on failure

Test it with functions that succeed and fail.
hint: function safeCall(fn, ...args) { try { return { success: true, data: fn(...args) }; } catch(e) { return { success: false, error: e.message, type: e.name }; } }

codeChecks.message: Return { success: true, data: result } on success.
Return { success: false, error: ... } on failure.

successMessage: safeCall wraps any function with error handling. This Result-type pattern (used heavily in Rust and functional JS) avoids exceptions in normal flow. Chapter 15 done!
